cap_basis: actual screening cost, with a statewide ceiling adjusted yearly for inflation
cap_summary: A landlord may only charge the actual out-of-pocket cost of screening, up to a CPI-adjusted maximum (about $62.02 in 2024). The unused portion must be refunded, and the landlord must give you an itemized receipt.
refund_rule: Any amount above actual cost must be refunded, and the full fee refunded if the landlord never obtained a screening report.
notes: The cap is recalculated each year by the CPI; confirm the current figure.

cap_basis: hard $20 cap on background + credit check
cap_summary: A landlord may charge no more than $20 total for a background and credit check — and must waive even that if you provide your own recent screening report.
refund_rule: Anything over $20 is unlawful; the fee must be waived entirely if you supply your own report and a background check from the last 30 days.
notes: Enacted by the 2019 Housing Stability and Tenant Protection Act.

cap_basis: application fees are not allowed at all
cap_summary: Massachusetts bars application and screening fees outright. At lease signing a landlord may only collect first month, last month, a security deposit (capped at one month), and the cost of a new lock and key — nothing else.
refund_rule: Any application fee is unlawful and fully refundable.
notes: Application fees are not among the four charges the statute permits.

cap_basis: application fees are banned
cap_summary: Vermont prohibits residential application fees entirely. A landlord may not charge you to apply or to be screened.
refund_rule: Any application fee is unlawful and fully refundable.

cap_basis: actual cost of obtaining the screening report
cap_summary: A landlord may charge only the actual cost of the tenant screening, and must first give you written notice of what the screening will cover, the criteria used, and the name of the screening company.
refund_rule: Charges above actual cost are not allowed; if the landlord doesn't run a report, the fee must be returned.

cap_basis: actual cost plus the reasonable value of the landlord's time, with limits
cap_summary: A screening charge can't exceed the landlord's actual cost. A landlord can't charge a fee when there's no available unit, must give a receipt, and must accept an applicant-provided screening report in many cases.
refund_rule: If the landlord doesn't process your application or no unit is available, the fee must be refunded.

cap_basis: actual cost or the average cost across applicants, whichever the landlord uses — and the same fee for everyone
cap_summary: Under the Rental Application Fairness Act, a landlord may charge no more than the actual cost of screening (or a documented average), must charge every applicant the same amount, must give a written receipt and disclosure, and must refund any amount collected above actual cost.
refund_rule: Any overage above actual cost must be refunded within 20 days; portable/reusable screening reports must be accepted.

cap_basis: $50 application fee cap, on top of the landlord's actual out-of-pocket screening cost
cap_summary: A landlord may charge an application fee of up to $50, separate from the actual out-of-pocket cost of a background/credit check and any refundable application deposit. If you're not approved, the fee (minus the landlord's actual costs and the $50) must be refunded.
refund_rule: If the applicant is rejected, the landlord must refund the fee within 20 days, less actual out-of-pocket costs.
notes: Distinguish the $50 application fee from a separate refundable application deposit.

cap_basis: the greater of $50 or 10% of one month's rent
cap_summary: An application fee may not exceed the greater of $50 or 10% of the monthly rent. If you aren't selected and the landlord didn't actually run a check, the fee is refundable.
refund_rule: Refundable if a screening was not performed; the cap is the greater of $50 or 10% of monthly rent.

cap_basis: $25, or the actual cost of a credit check if higher
cap_summary: A landlord may not charge more than $25 for an application/credit-check fee — unless the actual cost of the credit report is higher, in which case only that actual cost may be charged. You can also provide your own recent credit report to avoid the fee.
refund_rule: Charges above $25 (or actual credit-check cost) are not allowed; applicant-provided reports must be accepted.

cap_basis: fees over $25 are refundable, minus the landlord's actual expenses
cap_summary: If a landlord collects an application fee over $25 and you don't end up renting, the landlord must return the amount above their actual out-of-pocket expenses, with a written list of those expenses.
refund_rule: Amounts over $25 must be refunded (less itemized actual expenses) if you aren't offered or don't take the unit.

cap_basis: no dollar cap, but the fee must be returned if the screening wasn't run or someone else was chosen
cap_summary: There's no dollar cap, but a landlord must return your application screening fee if they did not run a background/credit check, or if they rented the unit to someone who applied earlier. Disclosures about the screening criteria are required.
refund_rule: Refund required if no screening was performed or a prior applicant was selected.

cap_basis: actual cost of the tenant screening report
cap_summary: A landlord may charge a processing fee for a tenant screening report only up to the actual cost of obtaining that report, and the fee can't be charged if the landlord uses a report the applicant already paid for.
refund_rule: Charges above the actual cost of the report are not allowed.
notes: UNCERTAIN: confirm the exact section and current language.

cap_basis: no dollar cap, but written disclosure and refund of any unused balance are required
cap_summary: A landlord must tell you in writing the amount of any application fee that won't be refunded and the purpose of the fee, and must return any portion not used for actual costs.
refund_rule: The non-refundable portion and its purpose must be disclosed; the unused balance is refundable.
notes: UNCERTAIN: verify the exact NRS section and refund language.

cap_basis: no dollar cap, but the landlord must give you its written rental-selection criteria
cap_summary: Texas doesn't cap the application fee, but the landlord must make its tenant-selection criteria available and notify you of the reason for any denial. If the landlord doesn't provide the criteria or an acknowledgment, the application fee must be refunded.
refund_rule: Refundable if the landlord failed to provide its tenant-selection criteria/acknowledgment.

cap_basis: no statewide cap
cap_summary: Florida has no statewide cap on application fees and they're generally treated as non-refundable. Your protection comes from general consumer law and the fee being reasonable for the actual screening.
refund_rule: No statutory refund right; rely on the terms disclosed and general consumer-protection law.
statute_citation: No specific application-fee statute (Fla. Stat. ch. 83, Part II)
notes: No cap — the markup gap is your leverage, not a statute.

cap_basis: no statewide cap
cap_summary: Georgia has no statewide cap on rental application fees. Fees are set by the landlord and are typically non-refundable; your leverage is whether the charge is reasonable versus the actual cost of screening.
refund_rule: No statutory refund right.
statute_citation: No specific application-fee statute (O.C.G.A. Title 44, ch. 7)

cap_basis: no statewide cap (some cities regulate)
cap_summary: Illinois has no statewide application-fee cap, though some local ordinances add protections. Statewide, your leverage is the gap between the fee and what screening actually costs.
refund_rule: No statewide statutory refund right; check your city's ordinance.
notes: Some municipalities (e.g., Chicago) add tenant protections — check local rules.

cap_basis: no statewide cap
cap_summary: Arizona doesn't cap application fees. The Residential Landlord and Tenant Act allows non-refundable fees if they're stated as non-refundable; otherwise amounts are presumed refundable.
refund_rule: A fee is refundable unless the agreement clearly states it is non-refundable.
notes: Non-refundable fees must be designated as such in writing.

cap_basis: no statewide cap
cap_summary: Pennsylvania has no statewide cap on application fees. Fees are landlord-set and typically non-refundable; the markup versus actual screening cost is your main leverage.
refund_rule: No statutory refund right.

cap_basis: no statewide cap
cap_summary: North Carolina caps several other rental fees but not application/screening fees. Those are set by the landlord and usually non-refundable.
refund_rule: No statutory refund right for application fees.
statute_citation: N.C. Gen. Stat. ch. 42 (no application-fee cap)

cap_basis: no statewide cap
cap_summary: New Jersey doesn't cap application fees by statute. The security-deposit law is strict, but application/screening fees are landlord-set; your leverage is the markup over actual cost.
refund_rule: No statutory application-fee refund right.

cap_basis: no statewide cap
cap_summary: Ohio has no statewide cap on application fees. Fees are landlord-set and generally non-refundable; compare the charge to the real cost of screening.
refund_rule: No statutory refund right.
statute_citation: No specific application-fee statute (Ohio Rev. Code ch. 5321)

cap_basis: no statewide cap
cap_summary: Michigan doesn't cap application fees. They're landlord-set; your protection is whether the fee is reasonable against the actual cost of the screening report.
refund_rule: No statutory refund right.
